lines: You sit by a Witherwood pine for a moment.
You realize you cannot remember what pine smells like.
Atrél has it now. He has set it on the altar between the others.
You write your save. As you do, you try to think of your mother saying your name.
You cannot hear it anymore. The sound is gone. The shape of her voice is gone.
Atrél holds it. He is gentle. You did not give it; it left you.
You stand at the Crossroads and try to remember which road you used to take
before this one. You cannot. You know there was another. The shape is missing.
Atrél receives the missing shape. He sets it with the others. He is full now.
You stop in the south aisle. There was a song you used to know. You hum the start.
The next note is gone. The song closes itself inside you, like a door at the end of a hall.
Atrél has it. The hall is shorter now. It still has rooms.
You write your save. You realize you no longer remember the name of the first
child who chose you back. The face is also going. The whole afternoon is going.
Atrél catches the afternoon as it falls. He has soft hands for that kind of thing.
You are reading. You reach for a word you have loved your whole life.
It is not there. You know its shape. You cannot say it. Atrél has the word.
It is on the altar between the others. He whispers it once for safekeeping.
Something hot brushes your left hand as the fight ends. You do not see what.
By the time you look, it is gone. The skin is white-pink where it should not be.
The hand will work. The hand will hurt. The hand is yours, in a different way now.
(-3 Max HP, permanently.)
You touch your temple, after. There is a white hair where there was none.
It is short. It is a child's grey. It will not change back. You leave it.
Atrél, who keeps such things, considers it an unbillable expense.
Something opened the skin under your jaw and the skin closed itself fast.
You did not notice when. The scar is a small ledge. Your fingers will find it
for the rest of your life. The next thing that swings at your face will glance.
(+1 Defense, permanently.)
After the fight, a back tooth is loose. You did not notice the blow that did it.
You touch it with your tongue. It will not go back. You will lose it later.
Atrél, who collects the small ones, has gentle hands for teeth.
(-1 Max Stamina, permanently — you sleep less well now.)
Your hands have finally gone hard where they need to be hard.
You did not notice. You notice now. Holding the weapon does not hurt today.
It will not hurt again. Your grip will tell the next thing you swing at.
(+1 Attack, permanently.)
You walk down the choir's nave and realize your right leg is slow.
It did not used to be. Something at the gauntlet took it. You did not notice.
The leg will work. The leg will be slower. You will sleep less to outwalk it.
(-2 Max Stamina, permanently. The Pall does not need to know.)
You are nearly down. You feel — and you know without seeing — that the Pall
has just looked at you, briefly, from somewhere very far away. The look is gone.
But you are now sure of something you did not used to be sure of. You are real to it.
You read. As you do, something in you wonders if you have been told a story.
Maybe Atrél is the kingdom's coping mechanism. Maybe the small altar is theatre.
You do not know what to do with the thought. The thought stays. It has joined you.
Something about this place — the air, the height of the ceiling, the unsung hymn —
decides for you that defending here is the wrong shape of action. You cannot do it.
You will not. Your body will refuse. The choir does not let you stand still.
(You can no longer choose Defend in the Choir.)
Walking away from the wolf you just killed, you become certain — without proof —
that this animal used to wear a collar. You can see the wear-line in its fur.
You will see it in every wolf you fight from now on. You will not be able to un-see it.
You crest a switchback and freeze. You cannot say why. Your legs simply
decide they are not going further today. You sit. The grey snow falls on you.
You will climb tomorrow. Today you cannot. The fear is yours now. It does not leave.
You walk into the Reach knowing — without checking — that the Engineer will
smile at you today. She does. You do not know why you knew. The knowing stays.
Atrél has filed it. It is the kind of thing he likes to keep.
The cat by the hearth, the same blue cat, has shifted. She is sitting closer to
where you usually sit. By a hand's width. She did not used to. She does now.
The cat will keep sitting at this distance. The distance is yours. Atrél will not have it.
A wolf — not the one you just killed; one watching from the trees — sees you.
She does not move. She knows you now. She will not be at the next Witherwood
fight you have. She will not be at any of them. You and she have decided.
(+1 Defense in the Witherwood, permanently. One wolf has chosen you.)
The Small Un, today, looks up when you walk in. They do not look up for everyone.
They have decided that you are someone. They will not forget. Atrél will not get
to forget you for them, either. You and the Small Un are written into each other.
The third tree from the road north has shifted very slightly. It is leaning
toward you. It is not the wind. The tree has been a tree a long time and
has decided. You did not earn this. It is yours anyway. Carry it gently.
After the fight, you pass Halna at her log. She does not look up. She does say,
to no one, that the next meat she dresses she will save you a cut. She means it.
The next time you arrive at her clearing, there will be a strip of meat on a stone.
(Halna will leave you a free Health Potion the next time you arrive.)
The Atrél-marker — Tessa's marker, you know now — registers your step the way
soil registers rain. It is not a magic. It is a smaller thing. The marker knows you.
When you pass it again, the stone will be a degree warmer than the air around it.
The cat by the hearth does not look at you today. She will not look at you again
this character. You did something. You do not know what. Atrél did not take this;
the cat decided. The cat is older than Atrél. The cat is allowed her decisions.
Halna will not nod at you when you pass her fallen pine. Not today. Not again.
The nod was a small thing. It is gone. You do not know which of the wolves she
feeds is the one that decided this. Halna does. She will not tell you.
You save. You realize, as you close the page, that you have not had a dream in
many nights. You used to. The dreams have left you. They will not come back.
Atrél has them all in a stack now. He has bound them. They are very neat.
The Insomniac is still counting on her beam. She is not counting you, today.
She used to. You used to be a dark mark. Today you are no mark at all.
She will not start again. The counting goes on without you. It is alright.
The Bellward's bell, that has been ringing your every hour since you arrived,
stops finding you. You hear it ring; the sound passes through you. It does not
ask. The Bellward has, somewhere in his fog, taken you off his list. You are sorry.
You realize, in the Gullet's wet, that you no longer have your left glove.
You do not remember where it went. The Older Hand will find it, eventually,
and set it among the one-of-each. You will not be there. You have walked on.
Your hand brushes a pebble on the path. It is warmer than the air. You pick it
up. It does not cool. You put it in your pocket. Atrél has not asked for it.
It is yours. It will not be in anyone else's pocket, this run or the next.
(A Warm Pebble has been added to your inventory.)
There is a small wooden button in the silt at your boot. Worn, bored through.
You pick it up. It was someone's, once. It is yours, in the smallest possible way.
Atrél holds the someone. The button is for you to carry. He has not asked for it.
On the path between the bakery and the Small Un's, a thread of dyed wool —
green, almost grey now. It must be the Small Un's. You pick it up. You knot
it once into the corner of your cloak. The third knot is for warmth, not show.
Tucked under a stone where you sat to read, a coin. Karst minting. The edges
soft. Someone's, once. Yours now. You will give it back, one day, if you find
where it goes. You will not find where it goes. You will carry it anyway.
On the south aisle's bench, where the column eats the word 'name,' a single
white feather. You take it. It weighs less than it should. The Choir does not
miss it. The feather is yours. Carry it. It is one small thing the Pall left.
Kerris's pack is open by the oven. There is a crust in it, set aside. He has
left it for you. He will not say so. He will not look up when you take it.
It will keep. It is from the bread Mira's recipe was for. It is enough for once.
(Kerris's Crust added — a one-use heal of 25 HP.)
You realize, three steps after the crossing, that the knot you tied in your cloak
for warmth is gone. The river took it. The river is patient. It tries everything.
Atrél has the knot now. He has set it carefully. It is shaped like a small house.
Counting your purse in passing, you find the smallest copper is gone.
You do not remember when it left. It was the one your father gave you,
or someone's father. You did not notice the absence until just now.
Atrél holds it. He holds everyone's smallest one. He is, in this, very rich.
You hum, while saving, a tune you have hummed all your life. The third note
is missing today. You try again. Still missing. You will not get it back.
Atrél has the third note. The rest is yours. The song still works, in pieces.
You taste the wet of the Gullet on your lips. There is no salt in it. You realize
you no longer know what salt tastes like. You knew this morning. You do not now.
Atrél has it. It will season nothing of his. He does not season. He only keeps.
Your left glove is gone. You do not remember when. The hand will be cold.
The hand will harden. The hand will be slower to block, until it learns.
Atrél has not asked for the glove. The Older Hand will find it. Eventually.
(-1 Defense, permanently — a small cold has gotten into you.)
You reach for the word kindness while reading. You cannot find it. You know its
shape; you know what it means; you cannot say it. Atrél has the word itself.
He has bowed. The kindness is still in you. It will work without its name.
Reading at Halna's stump, you decide — without saying so aloud — that you will
come back to it. Tomorrow, or the day after. Halna does not need you to. You
have decided. Promises are small. They are also kept. You are someone who keeps.
You read the wall behind one of the five chairs. You decide, quietly, that if
you reach the Summit you will say their name aloud. Just the one. You do not
say which. The wall does not need to know which. The wall has heard, anyway.
You walk past the hearth. You decide you will write a line in the book before
you leave this village this run. You do not need to tell the hearth-keeper.
She will know when you do it. She will not ask what you wrote. She will keep it.
You decide, at a small grave you have read, that you will lay one flower here
before this run ends. One. Small. The flower does not have to be impressive.
It only has to be set. You will set one. The dead get every flower offered them.
Walking past the bakery, you decide you will remember Kerris's name no matter
what Atrél takes from you. You write the name in your cloak's seam, in pencil.
It will smudge. You will write it again. Some names you decide to keep yourself.
You decide — quietly, walking past his altar — that next time you pass here,
you will set one small grief down. You know which. You will not name it now.
Atrél will be here. He has been here. He will accept it the way he accepts all of them.
You realize, on the climb, that you did not go back to Halna's stump. You
promised yourself you would. You did not. There is no harm done; it is a stump.
Halna sets fresh bread on it tonight, the way she sets it every night.
The breaking is small. It is yours. Atrél does not take broken promises. They stay.
(-2 Max HP, permanently. You will feel this. You meant well, once.)
You stand at the Summit. You forgot which name. You promised to say one. You
cannot. It is, of course, exactly the one Atrél will keep for you, now. The
wall in the Sealed Chamber will not mind. It has heard people forget before.
(-1 Attack, permanently. You meant to remember. You did not.)
You are bleeding. You remember, abruptly, the flower you promised to lay.
You do not have a flower. You do not have time. You will not have a flower
again this run. The promise is broken. The grave is the same grave. It waits.
You save. You realize Kerris's name is no longer in your cloak's seam. It must
have smudged out. You meant to write it again. You did not. Atrél has it now.
Kerris would forgive you. You will not ask him to. You will keep the silence.
You realize, far from the village, that you did not write the line. The book is
still on the hearth. It is still open. Someone else will write the next one.
The hearth-keeper does not mind. You mind. The minding is yours. It does not leave.
(-1 Max Stamina, permanently. The sleeplessness of small things.)
You save. You realize — too late, again — that you did not bring the grief to
Atrél's altar this run. You meant to. You meant well. Atrél's hands are open
anyway, in the gentle way they are always open. He is patient. You are not the
first promise-breaker. He has held this kind of forgetting many times. He will hold yours.
At the edge of your vision, in the gauntlet, a face. Just a face. You did not
see whose. You did not have time to look. The face is gone. You know it was someone.
You will not know who. Atrél files the almost-seeing. It is enough for him to file.
The river is speaking. You almost hear your name in it. Not your name; close to it.
You stop to listen. The river goes back to being water. The almost-hearing stays.
It will surface at odd moments. You will not be able to say which moments. It is yours.
Walking past Kerris's oven, you almost remember a smell that is older than you.
Your grandmother, maybe. Your mother. You do not have the smell — only the
almost-having. Atrél has the smell. The almost is yours. The almost is enough.
You almost understand what Atrél is doing. You almost see the shape of it. You
do not, in the end. The almost-understanding stays with you. It is a kind of
knowing. It is not the knowing. You will carry the almost. You will not say so.
On the climb, your foot decided — once, between two steps — to turn around.
You did not. You climbed. The decision is in you, though. You almost did. You
know you almost did. Atrél has the almost. The Pall does not have it yet.
You are nearly down. The grey, for half a breath, shifts in a shape that looks
like an old man bowing. You blink. The grey is the grey again. Hunger never bows.
The almost-bowing is yours. It is a heavy thing. Atrél nods at you. He does not bow.
Halna watches you walk past her fallen pine. She does not nod. She does not speak.
But your grip, when you pick up your weapon again later, feels older. As if she
passed her hands' knowledge through her watching. You did not ask. She did anyway.
(+1 Attack, permanently — Halna has taught you without saying so.)
Halna, after the fight, says one word to you. Quiet. You do not hear which.
You know it is a name. You know it is hers. You will not repeat it. The wood
will not let you. The word is yours now. It is also still hers. Words can be both.
Halna looks past you today. Tomorrow she will look past you again. You did something.
You may not know what. The wolf at the stump knows. Halna does not let you know.
The unseen will weigh in you. The wood is not on your side anymore. Not against you.
(-2 Max HP, permanently. To be unseen is a wound.)
On the flat stone by Halna's log, today, a strip of dressed venison wrapped in
leaves. She is not at the log. She has gone hunting. You take the meat. It will
hold. It will keep one of your wounds shut, later, when you need it to.
(Health Potion added to your inventory — Halna's promise, kept.)
Walking back past Halna's pine, you stop. She is sitting. She is not working.
She tells you, without looking at you, that her daughter went into the wood
thirty winters ago. She does not say more. You do not ask. The telling is enough.
You will carry this. You will not tell it. The wood is the only one who needs it.
You come back to the Engineer's bridge. Helka is standing. Her left arm is out of
the mud. The mud-line on her sleeve has darkened to dry. She is holding a beam.
She does not thank you. She nods once. She sets the beam into the third dyke's slot.
(+5 Max HP, permanently. Her vow has been kept. Yours has been honored.)
Helka shows you a knot. With her left hand, which is the one in the mud. She does
not lift the elbow. She uses the wrist only. The knot holds beams against rivers.
It will hold cloth against your skin, when something tries to open you, later.
(+1 Defense, permanently — the kitchen knot, kept warm against your bones.)
Helka, today, presses a chisel into your hand. Short, brass, worn smooth.
'I will not need this. I have the long one. Take it. Carve a name somewhere.'
You will. You will carve a name where the stone is hardest. The chisel will take it.
You walk into the Reach and the water flattens itself for half a heartbeat.
It does not always do this. It does this for you, now. You will walk less heavy
in the Reach. You will sleep less heavy after. The water has decided.
(+1 Max Stamina, permanently — the river has taken your weight.)
Helka does not look at you today. Her elbow is in the mud. You did not break the
Reaper. You did not come back. The beams are still at the dyke. The dyke is still
the third one. Helka is still where she was. The not-lifting is yours to carry.
The Lampkeeper looks up from his unlit lamp when you arrive. He has not looked up
for anyone, in a long time. He sees you. He says, quietly: 'I have written your
name. Mine is back too. We are both — for the moment — ourselves. Walk well.'
(+3 Max HP, permanently — to be seen by someone who has lost so much is a balm.)
One of the north-quarter lamps, today, catches as you walk under it. Just a flicker.
The Lampkeeper, who is far down the lane, does not see. You see. You did not tap it.
Lamps the daughter lit, once, sometimes remember. You will not say which lamp it was.
The Lampkeeper passes you, today, a folded paper. A child's drawing. A lamp. Tapped.
'My daughter drew this. I have kept it. I want one more person to have seen it.'
You look. You hand it back. He folds it again. You will remember the tap. He will not.
You save. You realize you no longer have the word for what the Lampkeeper tends.
You can describe it. You cannot name it. The word is at Atrél's altar.
The Lampkeeper, if you saw him now, would have to point. You would understand the pointing.
The Lampkeeper does not look up today. Or tomorrow. Or again. You did something.
You may not know what. The Pall took something from him you do not yet know about.
He may take it back. He may not. You will keep walking past him. He will keep tending.
The Old Penitent looks at you, today, from across the snow, and says one word.
You did not know he had a name for you. He has not had one. He has one now.
He will not say it again. You will not know it. The word is his. It is also yours.
After the fight, your breath does not come the way it did. The Climb is in your lungs.
It is not fatal. It is not leaving. The Penitent, far below, does not look up.
He knows. He carried this for forty winters. You will carry it for as long as you climb.
(-2 Max Stamina, permanently. The Climb is in you now.)
You write your save. The pencil tastes of ash. So does your mouth. So does the wind.
The Penitent's grey has gotten into your senses. It is not unpleasant. It is not pleasant.
It is the colour of where you have been. It will not leave. You will save again.
On the flat rock by the Penitent, today, a single scavver fang. Set carefully.
He has left it for you. He has not used it himself. He cannot taste anything now.
Take it. It is a small dead thing. It remembers small things best.
(Scavver Fang added — narrative-only item. Some things you carry are not for using.)
The Penitent, today, says one word to you. 'Maren.' His wife's name. He has kept
the word. He has given it to you. He cannot keep it any longer; the Pall is close
to his last names. He has chosen which one to hand off. You will keep it for him.
Tálva, today, sets a letter on the bench between you. From her great-grandmother.
The hand is steady. The chamber's air, sealed in, is still in the folds. You read.
Tálva does not ask what you think. Some things she does not need to ask about anymore.
(+1 Defense, permanently. The case is in you too now.)
Tálva presses the satchel into your hand today. The case, ready to carry.
She does not say go. She does not say take it. She does not say anything.
You walk out of the hold with it. The weight is uneven. It will become familiar.
The Small Un, as you leave, tugs the corner of your cloak. They have tied something.
It is a kitchen knot. You did not teach them. They have learned. They have tied
it into your cloak so it will stay with you. You will not undo it. Not ever.
(+2 Max HP, permanently — the third knot, in your cloak, kept for warmth.)
Kerris's pack is open again. There is bread set aside. He does not look up.
He has decided to save you bread on the days you come. Today is one of those days.
Take the crust. It will keep. It is from the bread Mira's mother taught her to bake.
(Kerris's Crust added — heals 25 HP once.)
The Small Un hands you a thread of charcoal. From their drawing of you. The one with
two faces. They do not say which face the thread is from. You do not ask. You will
carry the thread. You will not lose it. It is your two-faced day, in a thread.
Cael, when you arrive, has used the chisel. Above her name — KESS daughter of Aral
— there is a half-letter. Yours. She is preparing. She is not yet handing off.
'I have started writing it,' she says. 'In case. You may not sit. You may.'
(+5 Max HP, permanently — the seal has decided you are at least possible.)
Walking into the Bone Tomb today, the stone shifts very slightly to take your foot.
Not a great deal. A hand's width. The next time you set a boot here, it will fit.
You will not stumble in this room. The stone has decided to keep you upright.
(+1 Defense, permanently — the stone is on your side, by a margin.)
Cael, today, says her sister's name aloud. Out of the stone. You hear it. KESS.
She has not said it aloud in centuries. She is using your visit to say it.
You will carry the saying. Kess will be remembered, in you, for as long as you remember.
Cael whispers, through the stone, the first seal's actual name. NO-NAME's real one.
She did not have it. She has had it for fifty winters; she found it scratched into
the chisel handle. You will not say it aloud here. You will know it. It will weigh.
Cael's eyes do not meet yours today. She has decided you are not the one.
She is not angry. She is sad. She will keep sitting. You will keep climbing.
You will not become the seal. Someone else will. It is alright. It is alright.
Atrél lifts a hand a few inches as you enter. He does not look up. He is doing a small
rite. You happen to be in its path. He has, without meaning to, blessed you a little.
He realizes. He shrugs. He keeps doing the rite. You may walk out lighter today.
(+4 Max HP, permanently — a small god's accidental kindness, kept.)
Atrél, today, hands you a page. Folded. Unsigned. He says only: 'In case you find',
'someone who will need it. Do not read it yet. It is not for you. Carry it.'
You take it. You do not unfold it. You will know when. Atrél trusts your knowing.
You set the grief down on the altar. You do not say what. Atrél does not ask.
He bows. Just enough to be a bow. He puts the grief on the altar with the others.
You walk out into the cold of the Choir. The weight is gone. Some weights stay gone.
(+6 Max HP, permanently. The small rite is what it was made for.)
Atrél, when you arrive, says: 'I had a pain. I held it for a long time. I will not
hold it anymore. I am — small as I am — passing it back to the world. Not to you.'
'But the world is also you, here. I am sorry. I will be quieter for a while.'
(+2 Max Stamina, permanently — Atrél has let go of one. The room is lighter.)
Atrél, today, lifts a hand to stop you. 'Not today. I am too full. Bring me yours
again later. I am sorry. I am a small god. I have been carrying many small ones.'
You will come back. Or you will not. Atrél will be here either way. He has time.
The hearth-keeper, when you sit down today, sets an extra piece of bread on your
plate. She does not say a word. You eat. You feel it later, in a fight, where the
extra was. Bread becomes its eaters. The hearth-keeper did the kindness silently.
(Health Potion added — Gravewatch bread carried through your bones.)
The hearth-keeper looks at how you walk in tonight. She does not say anything.
She moves your usual cup an inch to the right, so you can reach it without leaning.
She has noticed. She has not made it a thing. She has only set the cup where it is needed.
(+1 Defense, permanently — to be quietly noticed is to be a little harder to break.)
The book is on the corner of the hearth. The pencil is on the mantel. You write.
You do not show the hearth-keeper. She does not ask. She knows you have written.
You may rewrite tomorrow. The book has room. The fire is the same fire. — Hearth-keeper.
You sit by the hearth and the fire, today, is a degree warmer than it should be.
You do not say so. The hearth-keeper does not say so. The fire knows. The fire is
the same fire it has been for the village's whole life. It has decided about you.
The book, today, is open. To the page with your line. The hearth-keeper has been
reading it. She does not say so. She turns a page when you sit down. You see what
page she has turned from. It is enough. She will go on reading it when you leave.
The Reader, today, looks up from the page. Briefly. The look is not for everyone.
It is for you. They nod once. They go back to reading. You will be a little harder
to break tomorrow. The pencil — Piranesi's pencil — has been writing your name.
(+3 Max HP, permanently. The Reader has marked you in their margin.)
The Reader, today, sets a folded paper at your spot before you arrive. A single line.
'I have been watching the way you walk. You walk like someone who is still here.'
'I have been here longer than the wood. Walk well. — Piranesi's older hand.'
You realize — without checking — that you have been in Piranesi's notes for some time.
Not by name. By gait. By when you arrive. By what you read. You are being kept track of.
You do not mind. The Reader is the gentlest accountant in the kingdom. It is alright.
The Insomniac, today, breaks her count to look at you. She tells you, quietly,
the number of climbers she has counted who looked like you. Five. You will sleep
less heavy tonight, for being five of someone. The counting goes on without your number.
(+2 Max Stamina, permanently. Counted, then released.)
There is a sixth blue mark on the Insomniac's beam, today. It is in a different blue.
'That one is for you,' she says, without looking up. 'It is a different blue.'
'You are not a cat. You are not a climber either. You are a different kind of beam-mark.'
The Reader's pencil, set on the desk corner, is pointing at you when you arrive.
Not quite at your chest. At your right hand. The Reader does not look up. The pencil
has been doing this since you arrived, you realize. You will not move it. It is fine.
You save. You realize the dream you had last night was narrated. In someone's voice.
Calm. Older than the road. The voice was describing what you were doing. From outside.
You will dream in this voice again, sometimes. It is alright. You are being read.
The Insomniac says, today: 'I have added a blue mark for your cat. It is the cat
you have been thinking of. I do not need to know which one. Some climbers',
'come with a cat in their head. I count those, too. I count many things. Yours is on.'
The Insomniac, today, stops counting when you walk in. She waits. She starts again
when you leave the room. She has paused on you. Not against you — pausing. She has
decided you do not need a count today. The not-counting is its own kind of held.
The Insomniac shows you, today, how to keep a tally on the back of your own teeth.
Quietly. With the tongue. She has done this for forty years. You will use it later,
in fights, to count what is left of you. The counting will keep you from losing track.
(+1 Max Stamina, permanently — knowing how to count yourself is a kind of rest.)
You are nearly down. At the edge of your sight, briefly, a face you have not seen
in many years. Your grandmother. She does not say anything. She does not need to.
She holds the door open. You decide not to walk through it. You straighten. You fight.
You survived. Just. The thing that nearly opened you carved a thin line down your side.
It will not heal back to where it was. The line is the line. You are a little smaller.
Atrél, who keeps the small ones, files it. He does not bow. He does not need to.

You breathed in a Mournhold that was — the one before the rite, the one that did not
yet need to forget anything. You forgot at once what that breath had carried. As it must.
You stood at the altar and Atrél, without asking, took a portion of one of your griefs
for the count of one slow breath. Then gave it back. It was lighter — not gone, lighter.
Atrél is patient with the weights you carry. You stand straighter under what is left.
(+1 Defense, permanently — a grief held briefly by another.)
You knelt at the altar today. The flagstone under your knee, after a count, was warm —
not from any candle, not from sun. From you, but back. The altar gave back what you gave,
with a little kindness added. Atrél does this rarely. Atrél did this for you tonight.
Atrél has a name from before the rite, an older one, the one the kingdom said before
they decided to forget. It almost came to you today. You almost said it. You did not.
It is gone again. But you almost. That has not happened to anyone you know.
You walked past the altar without kneeling. You were in a hurry. You had your reasons.
Atrél does not ask for the kneeling. Atrél notes it, though. Something in your chest
takes a small step away from the altar tonight. It will not step quite back.
(-1 Max HP, permanently — a kneel withheld is a kneel withheld.)
You stood at the altar for a long count without speaking. The altar stood with you. The
candle stood with you. The air stood with you. For a long count a small kingdom was
quiet because you were quiet. Mournhold is rarely quiet. Today, for a count, it was.
For the first time, the other way. Atrél, in the dark, set a small grief beside you —
the way you have set yours beside Atrél. You did not know whose grief. You held it
anyway. Atrél took it back. You stood taller. Atrél stood taller too. That was today.
(+3 Max HP, permanently — a grief held the other way.)
The shrine air is older than your lungs. You breathed it for a long count. When you
stepped back into the kingdom's air, your lungs took a beat to remember how to be your
lungs again. A small portion of the older air did not leave. You will breathe it now.
(-1 Max Stamina, permanently — older air, harder to let go.)
You stood at the last switchback today and the wind came down and almost talked you out
of the climb. For one count you were sure you would turn back. You did not. Something
in your chest gave a thread to that almost. The Summit takes the threads. It is patient.
(-1 Max HP, permanently — a thread given to the almost.)
The wind, at the last turning, said a single syllable that you understood. You will not
say it aloud — it is for you, the wind decided. It sits in your mouth now, the way a
stone sits in a pocket. You will swing harder for it. The wind chose you for this.
(+1 Attack, permanently — a word from the wind, kept in your mouth.)
You crested the last shoulder and for one breath the Pall, the kingdom's forgetting,
paused. As if it considered you. As if it was choosing whether you were worth the
trouble. It decided. The wind moved again. You will not know what it decided. Yet.
You expected rock. You found ash, and beneath the ash, ground that was softer than you
thought. As if the Summit were not a mountain. As if it were a thing that had once been
alive. You did not say that to anyone. You will not. But you walked the rest of it slower.
Somewhere on the last steps, a button came off your sleeve. You did not notice when.
You noticed after. It is one of the things the Summit will keep. You will not go back
for it. The wind will pass over it for a long time before it stops being a button.
On the last switchback the old footing — worn smooth by the previous Warden, who climbed
this many times — fit your boot as if it had been waiting for you. You stood in another
climber's place. They were not surprised. The Summit is patient with new feet. It has time.
Your breath, in the thin air, came out as a thread of steam and went, today, straight
into the Pall and did not come back. You felt the cold that did not come back, very
briefly, in your jaw. It hardened you a little. You will be harder to break now.
(+1 Defense, permanently — a breath given to the Pall.)
You said a name into the Summit wind. Not yours. Someone's. The wind took it without
comment. It is in the wind now. The wind passes over the kingdom every night. The name
will pass over the kingdom every night. You did that. You did not have to. You did.
On the climb today, very quietly, the Summit taught you your own name. Spelled different.
An older spelling. The one the kingdom used before the rite. You will not be able to
write it down. But you know it now. You have known it for a long time. You did not know.
Somewhere at the third switchback, a smaller you — a younger one — stopped climbing.
Sat down at the edge. You did not notice when. You went on. Whatever was small in you
is sitting on a rock at the third switchback now, and is not coming up after you.
On the second hook from the door, a single leather strap was set aside today — softened
with use, the right length for your wrist. As if a hunter, long gone, had set it back
for the next hand. Your hand. You took it. It steadies you when you carry weight now.
(+1 Max Stamina, permanently — a strap left by a hand you will not meet.)
Among the curled-black drifts of the Library, today, one page lay unburned. As if the
fire had stepped around it for you. You read it. You will not remember the words. You
will remember the shape of the reading. The Library kept one page for one reader: you.
You stepped across the Border today and the air, for one count, was the air of a country
that is not Mournhold. You breathed it. You did not know what country. You stepped back.
The air of Mournhold is the same as it was. Yours is not. You have crossed once now.
The outpost is abandoned. You know this. Today, as you came up the rise, you had the
exact feeling that someone was on the wall, looking for you, and lowering their bow
with relief at the sight of your shape. There was nobody. Someone was. You felt it.
You came into Wynne's camp tonight and the kettle on the cold ash was still warm. You
put your palm to it. Recently warm. Not by your hand. There was nobody. There had been.
You missed them by a count too small to name. You walked the rest of the night slower.
(-1 Max Stamina, permanently — a meeting you missed by inches.)
You put your hand to the Margrave's old seal today, the carved one, the one that means
a writ that nobody now honours. It softened in your palm — gave a little, as if it had
been waiting for a hand. The stone did not break. You did. A little. You can carry that.
On the third nail today there was a button. Bone, scored once. The other half of the
pair that came off your father's coat in the year you were small enough to ride his
shoulders. You did not know one half was missing. You know now. Take it. The cache nods.
You touched a page that was still, somehow, wet. Ink the colour of an old bruise came
away on your thumb. It dried there. It is not coming off. You will go on with it. It
is not large. Someone who looks closely will see it. They will not ask. You will not say.
At the Border crossing the old signpost, today, was not pointing along the road. It was
pointing at you. Not the road. Whatever quarter it had marked, you were the quarter now.
You did not feel honoured. You felt seen. You felt thinner by a thread. You will be.
(-1 Max HP, permanently — a thing pointed at you, that should have pointed elsewhere.)
The fire circle at Wynne's tonight had a small stone-free patch on the south side, the
exact size of you sitting cross-legged. You sat. The fire was cold. The patch was yours.
Whoever sets the circle has left a space for the next one in. The next one was you.
You saved tonight at an hour that was not your usual hour. The disk took the save. The
saving felt, faintly, the way a stranger feels — your own hand, but at a different
time of night. You will save at the usual hour next time. The disk noticed you noticing.
You sat to save and your breath, between one moment and the next, caught up with you.
You had not noticed you were short. You felt the length of your lungs again. The save
did not do it. The pause did. You will sit a little easier from now on, at every save.
(+1 Max Stamina, permanently — a pause that gave you back a breath.)
On the page today there was a line you knew. The exact words. You had not seen the page
before. The line was not, you would swear, on the page yesterday. The page kept it for
you. Or you brought it. Or it was always there, and you have always almost known.
When you closed the book the page you had been on was folded down at the corner. You
did not fold it. The page folded itself, for you, against the next time. It is a
small kindness. The page is keeping its place for you. You should come back to it.
You sat to save with a name on your tongue — one of the lost, one of the named-fallen.
The save took. The name took. The next time you sit at the disk, the name will be
there too, faintly. You have folded one of theirs in with one of yours. They will keep.
While you read tonight the paper, very faintly, moved with your breathing. As if the
page were also breathing, and trying to match. You let it. For one count you and the
page breathed together. You stood up a little less alone. The page is in this with you.
(+1 Max HP, permanently — a small companionship from a page.)
At the moment of saving tonight you remembered, all at once, exactly where you were the
last time you sat to save. The chair. The light. The thing you were thinking. The save
remembered too — gently, on your behalf. The act has begun to know its own history.
You sat to save tonight and you tried, briefly, to save only the parts you were proud
of. The disk took everything anyway — including the small wound you were trying not
to keep. The disk does not let you choose. You will not choose at the disk again.
On a page tonight you read a line in someone else's hand — a long-dead clerk, by the
loops of the letters — and the loops, faintly, were the loops you make when you write.
You have borrowed something of theirs without asking. You will write that way from now on.
There is dust in the corner of every save you make. Not much. The dust of all the runs
before this one — yours and the others'. It is gathering. You cannot wipe it away. You
did not put it there. It does not, in fact, belong to you. But it is in your saves now.
After the fight, you carried one of theirs out from where they had fallen. You did not
have to. The body was heavy. You held it differently than you had ever held anything
before. Something in your back learned a thing you will not unlearn. You stand straighter.
(+1 Defense, permanently — a weight your body now knows how to bear.)
At the worst beat, the shield-arm took a blow that should have been the killing one.
It took it. The arm has a new memory now — a deep one, in the bone. The bone aches
before rain, will ache before rain forever, and the rest of you has less room to breathe.
(-1 Max Stamina, permanently — the arm remembers, and the rest of the body pays.)
Your first strike of the fight, today, went somewhere it had not intended — past the
guard, low, where you usually never reach. The body of the enemy was where your blade
was. You don't know whose body had taught your hand that line. But it knows the line now.
(+1 Attack, permanently — a line your hand learned, in the dark, from somebody.)
Somewhere in the worst of it your haft cracked against stone. You did not feel it crack.
You felt it after — when the weight in your hand was wrong. The grip you trusted is
the grip you will not trust again. The kingdom takes such trusts. It does not give back.
(-1 Max Stamina, permanently — a trust withdrawn from your own hand.)
In the middle of it, a count came back to you — your old drillmaster's count, the one
they used to set the rhythm in the yard. They have been dead a long count themselves.
You held to the count. The fight went where the count went. You will stand to that beat.
(+1 Defense, permanently — a count given to you by the long dead.)
You stepped on a particular slick of moss today, and for one count a friend you lost a
long time ago stepped beside you. Not behind. Beside. As if they had not died, as if
the war had not. Your chest took a small price for that count. You will pay it again.
(-1 Max HP, permanently — a step you took beside someone who could not be there.)
Today, at a sound you did not understand, you drew your blade and there was nothing.
Just air. You sheathed it. The air remembered. You will not draw at a sound that small
again. But the air will. The air keeps a small record of every blade drawn against it.
A widow in Gravewatch thanked you tonight — not for anything you did this run. For a
thing your last warrior did, and the one before that. You did not know how to take it.
You said nothing. You both cried. She thanked you first. That has not happened before.
A buckle dug into your side for so long under the worst of the fight that, after, the
skin had folded around it. It became a scar in the shape of the buckle. The scar is
armor too, now — your body decided that, without asking you. You will be harder to kill.
(+2 Max HP, permanently — a scar in the shape of your own gear.)
Tonight, in the dark before sleep, you said a name to your blade. Not yours. Someone
you could not keep alive. The blade took the name. The next time you draw it, the name
will be on the steel. Steel takes names well. Steel cannot betray the names it takes.
(+1 Attack, permanently — a name given to your blade, in the dark.)
You brushed past someone today in the close street and felt — without taking — that
their pocket had set aside a single small coin for your fingers, the way a hand sets
aside a thing for a sibling. You did not take it. You felt seen. You walk lighter.
(+1 Max Stamina, permanently — a pocket that had room for you in it.)
A lock today opened under your hand at a pressure it had no business yielding to. You
tested it after. You could not make it open again. It opened once, for you, for the
thing on the far side. You took the thing. Your hands have a new sureness in them now.
(+1 Attack, permanently — a sureness a lock decided you deserved.)
Down the corridor today you heard the click of a closed door opening. Nobody else did.
You went and looked. The door was closed. It had not been opened. But you heard it open.
You hear those, now — the doors that almost did. You will hear them for the rest of this.
At the worst of it tonight the shadow under a beam, where you had stepped, did not
rustle. It should have. The shadow has decided to be quiet where you walk. You can feel
it deciding. The dark is in this with you. The dark holds its breath when you step.
(+1 Defense, permanently — the dark keeps its own counsel where your foot falls.)
Your boot, on the wet slate of the roof, today, held. It should not have. The slate was
old and rounded. You crossed it. You did not look down. You don't know what held you.
Something held. You will, in dark moments, wonder if it was the roof itself. It was.
Your throwing knife came back to your hand tonight warmer than it left. Not blood-warm.
Hand-warm. As if it had been in a stranger's pocket for a count, between you and the
throw, and was returned to you. You took it. You will not throw it again this run.
(-1 Max HP, permanently — a borrowed warmth that did not leave you whole.)
In the bad moment a lesson came back to you — a thief-friend you had once, in the year
before they were caught. They taught you to stay perfectly still and let the guard's
eye go past. You stayed still tonight. The eye went past. They kept a thing in you.
(+1 Max Stamina, permanently — a stillness taught to you, kept in you.)
You walked the market in Gravewatch tonight and four eyes that should have caught you
— eyes you can name, eyes you know — were elsewhere when you passed. Not coincidence.
The market has its own preferences. Tonight it preferred you to pass. You passed.
Tonight you took a small thing from a sill that, you realised after, was a thing of
yours — left there a long time ago, by you, when you were younger. You took it from
your own younger self. The thing is yours. You took it from yourself. You will pay.
(-1 Max HP, permanently — a theft from a self who could not protest.)
At the worst beat of the fight the enemy's palm — the one on the grip — relaxed. Not
a slip. A relaxing. As if they were tired of fighting you, particularly. You finished
them. They went down easy. You don't know why they relaxed. You will think about it.
Today, before any other thought, the first word of a spell you have not cast in years
came up to you. Whole. Clean. The way the very first words used to come, when you were
newer. You held the word. You will not say it now. But it is in your mouth, ready.
(+1 Attack, permanently — an old word, returned to you in good condition.)
The very first one. The one your teacher gave you, that little candle-lighting thing,
two syllables — you reached for it tonight and there was nothing. The page in you
where it lived is blank. Your teacher is dead. They cannot give it back. It is gone.
(-1 Max HP, permanently — your first spell, taken cleanly by the Pall.)
You thought, briefly, that you would like the lamp lit. The lamp lit. You had not said
the word. The kindling caught on the thought alone. This has happened a small number of
times in the history of the discipline. It has happened once to you. You said nothing.
At the bad moment, you looked down and the dust at your feet had — without any drawing
by you — composed itself into a small protective circle. A ward, freshly drawn, by the
ground beneath you. You stepped out of it after. It stayed in your gait somehow.
(+1 Defense, permanently — a ward the ground drew on your behalf.)
Reading tonight you came too close to the candle and a margin caught and you smothered
it but a corner of the page was gone, and on that corner was the close of a spell you
had loved. The spell is half now. The half is no spell. You are dimmer for it.
(-1 Attack, permanently — a spell halved by your own candle, your own hand.)
In the casting, mid-fight, a voice — your old mentor's, who has been dead the count
of a small kingdom — said *no, here* and your hand obeyed and corrected the angle of
the gesture. The spell took. You stood up after. They have not stopped teaching you.
(+2 Max HP, permanently — a correction from a teacher who is no longer there to give it.)
For one cast tonight, the air bent the way old air bent — old air, kingdom-old air,
the kind that is older than the rite that forgot itself. It did not resist your shape.
You will remember that compliance. Your hand will remember. Your spells will be cleaner.
(+1 Attack, permanently — an air that decided to be shaped, briefly, for you.)
A name came to you today — a familiar's name, the soft kind of name people give little
creatures who help them with the work. You did not have a familiar. You have never had.
The name went again. Some other you, in some other run, had one. The name was theirs.
You used a syllable tonight that did not belong to you. You used it because nothing
else would have worked. The syllable did the work and then took a small bite from the
inside of your mouth, in payment. Your breath will be shorter from now on. Justly.
(-1 Max Stamina, permanently — the cost of a word you could not have rightly known.)
The book opened, tonight, to the right page. You had not flipped to it. You had been
thinking about the spell on it — barely thinking — and the book, on its own, found
the page for you. A small companionship. Books do this for the people they know.
(+1 Max Stamina, permanently — a book that found your page for you.)
A bird in the low brush, today, did not lift at your step. It should have. It is the
kind of bird that lifts at the rustle of a vole. You walked close. It cocked its head
at you. You walked on. The brush has, for the moment, decided that you are not a threat.
(+1 Defense, permanently — a small judgement from the wild, in your favour.)
Where you walked today, the moss was a little thicker. The roots tucked themselves
down. The path did not catch your foot anywhere. Some paths take to some hunters. This
path, today, took to yours. You will walk it again. It will be ready for you again.
You knelt by the stream today and the bend of the water seemed to deepen, just enough,
to fit your cupped hand. You drank. The water was sweet. Hunters notice such things.
You will not say it out loud. But you will count the stream as one of yours, from now on.
(+1 Max Stamina, permanently — a water that gave you what you needed.)
You slept tonight in a copse where, by the prints, a wolf had passed. The wolf did not
come back. You slept the whole night. The wolf has, for this run, decided about you.
When you next see a wolf — and you will — you will look at it differently. It will look back.
(+2 Max HP, permanently — a sleep allowed you by something that owed you nothing.)
You loosed a shaft tonight and, after the fight, found it where it had fallen. There
was no flight-sound when it came back into the leaves. Arrows make a sound. This one
did not. You picked it up. The leaves did not crackle. The forest is being polite to you.
Whatever was on your trail today lost it at the thicket. The thicket closed behind you.
You did not push it shut. It shut. The thicket has decided whose side it is on. That
decision cost it a little of itself; you felt it cost you a little of yourself too.
(-1 Max HP, permanently — a kindness from the wild has its small price.)
At the worst moment you put a shaft at a shape in the brush, and the shape was not
there. Was, briefly, but had not been an animal. You spent the shaft. You spent the
breath. The forest noted the waste. Your draw will be a little harder, from now on.
(-1 Max Stamina, permanently — a shot the forest is keeping the cost of.)
On a ridge today you found a hunter's blind — freshly braced, the brush still green
in the weave. No hunter. The position was good for a southerly. You sighted from it.
The shot you would have taken was clean. Someone left it for you. You took the place.
(+1 Attack, permanently — a sight-line another hunter prepared for the next pair of eyes.)
The thing you were hunting, tonight, stopped at the bad moment and gave you the breath
you needed for the killing shot. It did not run when it should have. Hunters know when
the quarry has, for whatever reason, decided to give itself. This one decided. You took.
You crossed a track today: boot-print same length as yours, same heel-bite, going the
other way. Fresh enough that you should have met the walker. You did not. The wood was
open. There was nobody. Some other you, in some other run, walked this way. Towards you.
In the worst beat you said a prayer you barely meant — half-thought, between the breath
and the strike. The prayer was answered. Not loud. Not by a name you would recognize.
But the strike landed. You stood. Something heard. Something will hear, from now on.
(+2 Max HP, permanently — a prayer answered in the way prayers are sometimes answered.)
You passed a grave today without pausing. You knew you should pause. You did not. The
grave did not call after you — graves never do. The graves you pass without praying
for sit a little heavier on you. From now on, that one will sit on you. You let it.
(-1 Max HP, permanently — a prayer withheld from someone who could not ask.)
Out of your mouth at the end of the fight tonight came a benediction in the older
tongue — the one before the rite, the one nobody has been taught for generations. You
did not know you knew it. The kingdom knew. The kingdom gave it back to you, tonight.
(+1 Attack, permanently — a benediction in a tongue older than your teaching.)
You stood at Atrél's altar tonight and Atrél did not speak. The silence was louder than
any speech. As if Atrél, for you, were doing something more attentive than speaking.
You did not need a word. You knew it had been given. You stood differently after.
(+1 Defense, permanently — a silence given to you in place of a word.)
One of those who fell tonight, in their last breath, said your name. Not asked for help.
Said your name, the way a child says the name of someone who is staying with them. You
stayed. You held the hand until the breath was done. The name is yours from them now.
(+2 Max HP, permanently — a name held by someone who could not keep their own.)
Mid-prayer tonight you went down. The words went with you. Not just the words you had
been saying — words behind them, words you had not yet thought of saying. The prayer
collapsed inward. Some of it is not there anymore. You will be quieter from now on.
(-1 Attack, permanently — a prayer collapsed, taking a corner of you with it.)
Tonight you blessed a door in Gravewatch as you passed it — quietly, almost without
thinking. The door took the blessing. The hinge will be easier from now on for whoever
opens it. You did not know whose door. It does not matter. The door knows.
For one count today you felt, distinctly, that a god — not Atrél, not one of the kind
ones — turned its eye briefly to you. You did not look back. You did not move. The
count ended. The eye went elsewhere. You will not say which god. You will not forget.
You sat at the disk to save tonight and, before saving, held a silence with someone
you could not see. It was prayer. You could feel that it was. The silence took a small
weight out of you. The weight had to come from somewhere. Your lungs paid the count.
(-1 Max Stamina, permanently — a silence that was prayer, paid for by the body.)
You stood among the silenced graves tonight and gave them, one by one, the blessings
that had been refused them in life. Halfway through, you felt one of them give you a
blessing back. Soundlessly. The way the silenced bless. You stood the whole night with it.
(+3 Max HP, permanently — a blessing returned by one who was forbidden to speak.)
At the end, an antler-tine caught a thread of your sleeve. You pulled away. The thread
stayed on the antler. The thread is still there. You can think of it sometimes. It
steadies you. The Pallid Stag was a deer once. The deer remembers being a deer for you.
(+1 Defense, permanently — a thread you left in something that used to be a deer.)
When the Stag breathed near you, in the bad moment, the breath smelled — for one count
— of cold grass. The grass it ate when it was a fawn. You smelled it. Nobody else did.
Nobody you can ask. You are the only one who knows what the Stag was, before. That is enough.
The last blow you struck the Stag was kinder than you intended. You aimed for the
throat. Your hand turned the cut at the last moment so the breath went out clean. The
Stag thanked you with its breathing out. You will not forget the way it thanked.
(+1 Max HP, permanently — a kindness given at the end, kept by both of you.)
It telegraphed twice before the heavy. You learned to defend on the second wind-up,
not the first. Your body remembered. Your body will remember the pattern against
every telegraphed thing from now on. The Stag was a teacher. You learned. You can.
(+1 Defense, permanently — a pause the Stag taught you in the rhythm of its grief.)
Halfway through the fight there was a moment the Stag could have run. It did not. It
had decided to die there. With you. You finished it. You don't know whether that was
kindness or you. Either way, you carry the choice now. The choice has a small weight.
(-1 Max HP, permanently — a choice you took that was not yours to take.)
The Reaper's scythe came across your jaw before you knew the blow had started. A line
of cold on your chin. Not a scar you can see in still water. A scar your jaw knows is
there. The Reaper drowned the fields. The Reaper has now also taken a thread of you.
(-1 Max HP, permanently — a line traced by what drowned the kingdom.)
Mid-swing, for one beat, the Reaper paused — looked at you — as if it had thought you
were someone else. Then it swung. The pause was real. The Reaper's grandchild, if it
had one, would have looked like you. You will not say this to anyone. You know it.
When it fell, the Reaper said one word — the name of a grain. The first grain the
fields lost when the floodgate was shut. You did not know the name. You know it now.
You will not say it. The grain hears its name even when it is not said. It will grow again.
(+1 Max Stamina, permanently — a grain named at the death of the one who drowned it.)
At its belt were the keys to the floodgates of the Sodden Reach. Rust-welded. Useless
now — the gates are open. You took one anyway. The key fits in your pocket as if it
had always belonged. The Reaper would not have minded. The keys are with someone again.
(+1 Defense, permanently — a key that knows it has come home with someone who will keep it.)
When the Reaper went down, the water in the weir, today, did not stir for him. It
should have. It used to know him. It did not even ripple. The water is done with the
Reaper. The fields are not. You walked back across them slower than you came in.
She braced. She always braced. Tonight, the brace came late — half a beat. You landed
the blow in the half-beat. You did not deserve that half-beat. You took it. Your hand
has a new confidence in finding a half-beat. It will find them in other things now.
(+1 Attack, permanently — a half-beat the Maw-Mother gave you, knowingly.)
Mid-fight she called a name. Not yours. A child's name — soft, full of vowels. She had
had one. She had eaten and forgotten the one she had. The name came up again because of
you. The Pall would have prevented it. The Pall failed. She named her child. You won.
At the very end she had air enough for one more blow. She used it to breathe out, not
to swing. The breath came toward you — warm, quiet. She gave you the room. You took
the breath in. You will have an extra count of breath, from now on, when you need it.
(+2 Max HP, permanently — a breath given by a mother who chose to give it.)
When she went down her hands curled in toward her chest — the way a mother's hands
curl when she has just put a child down. You saw it. The Maw-Mother had been a mother,
had been, had been. Her hands remembered last. You will dream of those hands. You can bear it.
When she went, a thin appetite she had been carrying — the Pall's appetite, given a
mother-shape — looked for a new place to go. It went, in part, into you. Not all of
it. A thread. The thread is in you now. You will eat a little more. You will eat slower.
(-1 Max Stamina, permanently — a thread of appetite the Pall could not let die.)
When the Bellward fell, the bell at the south door of Mourncross rang once. Not by
any hand. Once, and a long count of silence. The bell rang for the Bellward. It also
rang for you — you were the one who had freed it from its keeper. You stood straighter.
(+1 Defense, permanently — a single ring of a long-silent bell, given to you also.)
The Bellward was Eron's father. You knew this. The Bellward did not, anymore. When he
fell, you said, quietly, *Eron asks after you.* The Bellward's face went, for one count,
less hollow. Whatever bound him slackened. You did that. Eron will know one day.
(+2 Max HP, permanently — a sentence given to a hollow man who had a son.)
A corner of his robe caught in your buckle when he fell. You tore it free. A small strip
of black cloth still in your buckle now. It will not come out. You stopped trying after
the second night. The cloth has decided to come with you. It weighs almost nothing.
(-1 Max HP, permanently — a strip of a hollow man's robe, riding on your belt.)
Below half he flipped — heavy, telegraphed strikes, the kind a father teaches a son to
block. He was teaching, then. Mid-fight. He was teaching the blocks he should have
taught Eron, and didn't, because he had been hollowed before he could. You learned.
Wound around his wrist was a short length of bell-rope. You took it. It rests in your
palm when you make a fist around it. Whoever holds the rope holds, faintly, the bell.
The bell rings differently for you now, when you pass under it. The town has noticed.
(+1 Max Stamina, permanently — a length of rope that knows the bell it came from.)
Cantor Vael led the rite that unremembered the kingdom. At the end of the fight tonight
Vael did not sing. Vael had nothing left to un-sing with. Vael's mouth was open. Nothing
came out. The silence into which the rite had spoken was Vael's, last, alone. You won there.
(+1 Attack, permanently — a victory at the throat of the one who unremembered.)
When Vael fell, one of the words the rite had taken — a single, common word, a word
for *bread* in the old tongue — came back into the air of the Choir. You heard it. You
could not have known it before. You know it now. The world has its bread back, by one word.
(+2 Max HP, permanently — a word the kingdom had lost, returned by your hand.)
When Vael went down the priest's robe went brittle — burned dry, but no fire. The robe
had been older than was right. The age came out of it. Some of the age, in coming out,
passed through you on its way to nowhere. You felt the years go through. You are tired.
(-1 Max Stamina, permanently — an age that passed through you on its way out of a robe.)
Because you carried the third line of the Verse into the Choir, Vael — at the end —
did not unsing it. Vael could have. Vael did not. Vael, the un-singer, kept one line
for you. For your line. You will keep the line longer for that. Vael held back. So did you.
(+3 Max HP, permanently — a verse kept by the un-singer at the end of the un-singer.)
In the worst beat Vael's gaze touched your face and you felt — for one count — your own
name begin to leave you. Like a thread being pulled out from inside. You held on. The
name stayed. Some of the thread is still loose. You will tuck it back in. You will know.
On the stone where the Penitent fell, you put your hand to steady yourself, and your
palm fit, exactly, into a handprint that was already there. Theirs. The first climber's.
Hand under hand. Two of you, briefly, in the same place. You stood up taller after.
(+1 Defense, permanently — a handprint your palm fit into, knowing whose it was.)
Every third turn they struck. You learned to endure those turns. You learned not to
trade. You learned to outlast a thing that did not tire. You can outlast more, now. You
will. The Penitent had been the first to turn back. The Penitent, in death, gave you the climb.
(+1 Max HP, permanently — an endurance the Penitent taught you in their relentlessness.)
Mid-fight you saw — clearly, briefly — the choice the Penitent had made. The first climber
who turned back. The same choice was there in front of you. You could turn back. You
did not. The choice has not gone away. The choice will be there at the Summit, too.
Before they went down, the Penitent said — quietly — the older name of the Summit. The
name the kingdom used before the rite. The name you have known and not known. You took
the name into you. You will not say it aloud yet. But you will, when it matters most.
When they fell, a flake of the ash that had been their skin came across your mouth.
You swallowed without meaning to. The ash went down. It is in you now — a small portion
of someone who once turned back. You will be a little slower to turn back. And to breathe.
(-1 Max Stamina, permanently — a flake of the first turning-back, taken into your body.)
Before the final beat the Warden — the previous Warden, your last victor — set their
foot down. Your foot, on its mirror-step, landed in the print they had left. Hand under
hand. Foot under foot. They had wanted the cycle to end here, with someone like you.
(+1 Defense, permanently — the Warden's footing taught to yours, beat by beat.)
When they fell, the Warden — without speaking, the Pall takes the speech — looked at
you and the keeping passed. They had carried the weight. They could put it down. You
would carry it. They thanked you with the breath they had left. You thanked them back.
(+3 Max HP, permanently — a keeping handed to you, hand to hand, at the Summit.)
The Pall reached for your name at the end. It was its right. It did not take it. Not
yet. Some part of the Pall — a part that had been the kingdom, that had been kind —
left your name for you to keep, this run. The next Warden will not get the same mercy.
In the last beat you and the Warden shared, briefly, one thought. You don't know whose
it was. It was the thought of a small light at the bottom of a staircase. You will see
that light, in dreams, for the rest of this run. It was a kindness. It was also a goodbye.
At the end of it, the Warden's breath and yours were the same breath, for one count.
Not a kiss. A trade. One of yours went into them. They took it down with them. You will
be short of one breath from now on. They will not be short of any. You can carry the loss.
(-1 Max Stamina, permanently — a breath traded with the Warden at the kingdom's roof.)
When the Dyke Warden went down, the water behind the dyke they had kept did not, today,
come over. It paused. As if out of respect. You walked across the dyke dry-footed. The
water remembered the Warden who had held it back. The water now, briefly, knows your face.
(+1 Defense, permanently — a water that learned to wait, for once, in your honour.)
On the dyke-keeper's belt was a small lantern, the kind that does not, properly, go
out in wind. You took it. It rides on yours now. You will not need to light it. It is
lit. It will be lit. The dyke-keepers' lanterns are like that. The keeper let you have it.
(+1 Max Stamina, permanently — a lantern that does not go out, taken in trust.)
Mid-fight there was one beat where they could have let you pass. They almost did. They
remembered their post in the next count. The post was the point of them. They came back
to it. You finished the fight. You will think about that beat. You can carry it.
After, you knelt at the post and with your knife — or with the edge of one of theirs —
scored a single letter into the stone of the dyke. Their first initial. Someone will,
one day, ask whose. There will be someone to tell. You stood up. You went on.
When the Warden fell, the water — held back so long — got a finger between the stones
and into your boot. Just a finger of it. But cold. Cold the way held water is, when it
has been held a long time. Your foot is colder now than the other. It will stay.
(-1 Max HP, permanently — a finger of long-held water that decided to come with you.)
When the Library Warden fell, a single page came loose from one of the heaped books and
spiraled down onto you. You caught it. You will not be able to read it — the ink is
gone, the paper is cold — but it is folded in your coat now. Pages take to their readers.
(+1 Attack, permanently — a page the Library let go to you, alone.)
When the Warden went down, the burned hall became — for one long count — perfectly
quiet. The ash stopped settling. The wind in the broken windows stopped. The Library
held silence for its keeper. You held silence with it. You stand quieter from now on.
(+1 Defense, permanently — a quiet given to you by a place that had been loud once.)
There was ink on the Warden's collar — fresh, the day's ink, not the burn's ink. They
had still been writing. Whatever they had been writing, the Pall took it. You know they
were writing. The room knows. The room will keep going on for a long time without them.
After, you knelt and laid one of the unburned pages over the Warden's eyes. You said
the name of the book you had borrowed last, in their hearing, as a kindness. They could
not have heard. The book heard. The book will keep the borrowing on the shelf for you.
(+1 Max HP, permanently — a borrowing the Library has decided to keep open for you.)
Library ash got into the fold of your coat tonight. It is fine — finer than wood-ash.
It will not come out. It rides with you now. Every time you put the coat on, you will
be putting on a part of the Library. You did not ask for it. You will not refuse it.
(-1 Max Stamina, permanently — a small weight of burned pages, kept by your coat.)
The Forgotten Thing — the one the Pall had unremembered so completely it became its
own unremembering — looked at you, at the end, and was the first thing to remember you.
Even before you remembered yourself for the day. You felt the remembering land. You stood up.
(+2 Max HP, permanently — a remembering given to you by the most forgotten thing in the kingdom.)
When it fell, the Forgotten Thing — which had been a word, before it had been a thing —
gave the word back. To you. You don't know which word. You will, sometimes, use it
without knowing. People will look at you. They will recognize it. You will not.
(+1 Attack, permanently — a word given to you by the thing that had been a word.)
When the Thing went, its silhouette — the shape of what nobody could remember — was for
one count visible, against the wall, like a shadow against a lamp. You saw it. You
did not know its shape. You will, very rarely, see that shape again in unrelated places.
On its way out the Thing took a thread of you — not a memory, exactly, more a small
habit of yours, the way you used to clear your throat before speaking. You don't clear
your throat anymore. You did not notice when you stopped. You won't get it back. It went.
(-1 Max HP, permanently — a small habit of yours, taken by the thing on its way out.)
It died, and the kingdom — for a count — did not yet know it had died. Because it had
been forgotten. The kingdom would not, ever, know it had died unless you told. You will.
You will, in pieces. You stood straighter, with the telling waiting in you, ready.
(+1 Defense, permanently — a telling you are carrying for a kingdom that cannot ask.)
